posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
I don’t buy white burgundy to cellar anymore as I was burned too many times and don’t really feel like taking the risk when there are other good options out there (thanks, Nahe and Rheinhessen GGs). But I do get to taste a fair amount of young white burgundy. My take is that in many ways, the premox “solution” at this point is almost as bad as the problem. Setting aside the closure debate, the wines are made in an early-drinking style where everyone seeks the Coche Dury matchstick and a wine that is generally delicate, refined and a wine that I would think of subtle and elegant. Those may not seem like bad things, and they aren’t. But they aren’t the way I would describe the Leflaive’s of the early 90s, or the other white burgs that made my mind explore in the early aughts, with tropical flavors, flowers, mushroom, earth, a wine that would hold its own with the reds and that was every bit as brawny and intense as a wine could be. When I open white burgundy young today, I just don’t see it developing into the same thing that I drank before premox, and that I was so sad to loose. I hope I’m wrong, and I have no dog in this fight as I’ve liquidated in one way or another all but about 20 bottles of white burgundy from a collection that once had a few more zeros on that number, but when I pop one today I don’t feel like I’m missing much by not buying them.
Alex

I feel the same way. Speaking broadly, I tend to like racier, more acidic wines. But the trend has gone so far toward Arnaud Ente-style lasers that White Burgs have lost some of the opulence and grandeur that has made top tier white Burgs among the world’s grandest wines.

I’ve had great bottles under DIAM from Jadot and others especially from Chablis but none at 20 years and for me that will be the true test whether the wines evolve like pre-95 white burgs with indelible flavors that in my burg and wine drinking experience are singular, the finest and by far the most pleasurable beverages ever produced.
I’m beginning to feel guilty opening bottles of my old treasures but “this is not a dress rehearsal.”
As Alan knows, I was ahead of the curve in acquiring/cellaring Blanc de Blancs Champagne with many entering into and or already in to the zone of peak drinkability.
That’s today’s reality, however.

As an aside, Recently I shared at an after party of Le Fete du Champagne and a Rodolphe Peters dinner in NYC, a bottle of 85 Batard made by Richard Fontaine under the Blain Gagnard/ Delegrange-Bachelet label with Rodolphe, Alex Chartogne, and other Champenois’ that was mind bogglingly profound. We were all pretty speechless in uniformly and silently acknowledging that the heights that this bottle hit, would be something else for the Champenois’ to aim for.

Cheers-

Stay safe to you all

It’s an interesting question about what the wines are generally like today versus before premox started. My amateur perception is that the wines are more open, less intensely acidic, and need less age than they once did.

Now that some producers may be dependable to age due to DIAM, I’m less sure which wines I should be trying to age and for how long. This is going to be an interesting new learning curve.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It’s an interesting question about what the wines are generally like today versus before premox started. My amateur perception is that the wines are more open, less intensely acidic, and need less age than they once did.

Interesting question. I think your observations are correct, but also that the wines, at least when they open up, are less rich, muscular and dramatic. Post #5 above makes some salient points in that regard.

Climate, and viticulture’s failure to evolve in tandem with climate (that’s changing, happily), clearly has plenty to do with it; but so I think does technical “progress”. Look at Jasper Morris’ 1988 (in white Burgundy terms, is that antediluvian or prelapsarian?) White Burgundy book where the up-and-coming producers are all talking about elegance and what they have learnt in the New World (caveat: I last read it in the Bodleian Library in 2012, so that may be a caricature). For many, that meant gentler, whole cluster pressing and clearer musts—something the arrival of pneumatic presses, easy ways of measuring turbidity, and clarification enzymes made much easier to realize. In retrospect, was that the right direction? This is a question that I think a lot of thoughtful winemakers today are pondering.

posts.name: John Morris

I had an interesting chat with Doug from Arrowine a couple weeks ago. He swears that the premox issue is because producers don’t load up on the sulfur like they used to. Pretty insightful thought. I don’t know quite enough to argue either way.

It clearly isn’t just one factor. At this point, it seems to be some coincidence of factors, including:

(1) the trend toward reduced sulfur usage,
(2) picking riper fruit with lower acids,
(3) other winemaking changes (for example, how you deal with lees; do you let the must go brown before fermentation, which can eliminate some of the precursors to the oxidized compounds) and
(4) the inconsistency of natural corks (which introduces the wild variability).

This seems like the best explanation, but it’s also a clever shorthand for “we really don’t know what causes premox so we are just going to list many of the different choices that go into winemaking as potential causes.” At least its better than the early years, when the response was “your just imaging it” or “that’s only happening in America”. I suppose that, given the range of choices that producers have to deal with the issue right now, “we don’t know but we are doing lots of things that we think will help” seems like a good enough answer. But it’s not without risk, both as to whether or not the wines will still stand the test of time, and equally as important - what they will be like over the course of their lives and at maturity.

One thing that seems clear is that for those who like the way white burgundy is made now and want an early-drinking style, it is a golden age! For those who preferred the wines before premox and thought that 15-25 years was the right time to approach, the casino is always open.

I see a lot of people discussing the stylistic change as a large factor here, which i think makes a lot of sense. They just wouldn’t have the same gumption being made in a gentler way.

Just opened a '15 Germain Meursault-Charmes this weekend I bought on release, and was so pissed when it was premoxed. Not sure this negates the point of how wine making style is a cause, but from my understanding Germain winemaking is “classic” Meursault. Anyway, it had just started to really oxidize and you could still tell how great the wine could/should have been.

Yes, Germain is an example of a producer that does pretty much everything right. But you can do everything right, and still be let down by the closure.

This is one of the more perplexing pieces to the puzzle because it still leaves us with the question of how the frequency of being let down by the closure increased exponentially starting almost immediately circa 1995. Certainly one would expect a producer doing everything right not to have any higher premox incidence than they did before 1995. You’d also expect that if hundreds of producers up and down the Cote simultaneously started doing different things circa 1995, it wouldn’t be so difficult to put a finger on what those things were, and then start, y’know, not doing them.

I think the best explanation is a decline in the quality of the closures (not necessarily visually, but in terms of density and OTR) happening at around the same time as winemaking changes made the wines a lot more fragile.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Could corks really have declined so precipitously between 1993 (almost no premox) and 1995 (epidemic of premox)? Especially without leaving much of a premox trail in other countries, including countries like Germany notorious for using lousy corks?

I do think that the 1995/1996 distinction is a bit too cleanly drawn: there are plenty of premoxed 1994 white Bordeaux, as well as 89, 90 and 92 white Burgs that evolved faster than one would have expected.

But the real point is that the winemaking changes, delivering much less robust wines, threw the decline in cork quality into relief. If you go from 55 ppm free SO2 at bottling to at most 20 ppm, and then a bunch of that is eaten up because you bottled from tank with too much dissolved oxygen, your wine is going to evolve a lot quicker than if you use the same closure you used before. And what before would have been “bottle variation” becomes something much more obvious.

It would be worth looking into changes in cork farming, harvesting, and processing around that time. It’s something I’d like to study more myself, as what I know is mainly anecdote from cork producers. But my understanding is that the trees were being harvested on a shorter rotation, and people were irrigating. Open to correction on this, but it would make sense in the context of surging demand for cork due to New World winemaking growth in that era.

Where is the testing and the data to support that hypothesis when comparing a statistically significant set of early 1990s and post 1993 corks (even up to present time), including the geographic nature of the issue so that France / Burg (but also Bordeaux and Alsace) have been affected but I haven’t seen any systemic issue in Germany and Austria? On top of that, there’s huge demand for the best cork, as well as growing demand for cork from DIAM and others producing composites who have a lot of clout in the market compared to some of the smaller premium cork merchants.

Well that Genevrieres story is certainly demoralizing. I don’t know your specific tastes to know which of 4 or 5 producers I can think of off the top of my head you might consider best, but to think those wines are basically DOA is ridiculous. Ugh.

I have more confidence in the 50-77 year old Huets I was just lucky enough to acquire.

Happily it was replaced. But it is also a producer who spares no expense on buying the best possible corks they can find (1.80 EUR per cork) and inspects each and every one before bottling. At that stage, the winemaker can’t do much more.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: The long-term issue with Diam basically is the binder and its stability. If you look, a lot has been discussed about the binder materials. Certainly Diam can achieve more uniform density and OTR, and achieve better quality control with tighter tolerances, than natural cork. I don’t really think that’s debatable. The question then is how chemically inert and stable the binder is (or the different binders are — they have evolved and AFAIK there are now two basic options, synthetic and beeswax-derived) over decades. It’s hard to simulate the binder experiment of what happens under various environmental conditions over a decade or two. I’m guessing the company has had certain experiments going for 15+ years now.)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Could corks really have declined so precipitously between 1993 (almost no premox) and 1995 (epidemic of premox)? Especially without leaving much of a premox trail in other countries, including countries like Germany notorious for using lousy corks?

I go back to my question. Have you had a premoxed white Burgundy finished with DIAM? The problem likely had a number of causes and likely was present before 1995, but in my somewhat limited experience with wines finished with DIAM, I have not had a premoxed wine.

No, but this merely shows that cork is an essential factor but not the sole factor, and it only solves the problem if producers aren’t too stubborn to switch to Diam, which, apparently, most are.

Absolutely.

I think one reason this debate gets so heated at times is that we are dealing with two separate but interrelated issues as if they were one. The first is the issue of oxidation, which can be solved by screwcaps and DIAM. The second is the issue of stylistic change, both because of winemaking and climate, which surely contributed to the oxidation issue, but also really changed the profile of the wines themselves. Anyone who is nostalgic for the glories of white Burgundy of yesteryear has to be interested in the second issue, which is in many respects more complicated, as it is not just a question of problem and solution.

I go back to my question. Have you had a premoxed white Burgundy finished with DIAM? The problem likely had a number of causes and likely was present before 1995, but in my somewhat limited experience with wines finished with DIAM, I have not had a premoxed wine.

No, but this merely shows that cork is an essential factor but not the sole factor, and it only solves the problem if producers aren’t too stubborn to switch to Diam, which, apparently, most are.

Absolutely.

I think one reason this debate gets so heated at times is that we are dealing with two separate but interrelated issues as if they were one. The first is the issue of oxidation, which can be solved by screwcaps and DIAM. The second is the issue of stylistic change, both because of winemaking and climate, which surely contributed to the oxidation issue, but also really changed the profile of the wines themselves. Anyone who is nostalgic for the glories of white Burgundy of yesteryear has to be interested in the second issue, which is in many respects more complicated, as it is not just a question of problem and solution.

The relative tedium of much modern day white burgundy is not due to premox but to the fact that the recipe appears to have been lost, the 90s fetishisation of ripeness, low yields, gentle pressing and a generally superrefined approach having been a disaster from which many producers have not recovered. With the changes in weather and the fixation with more ‘natural’ ways of producing wine it appears that rather few producers yet have a way forward and that those that do produce wines so vastly sought after that only those with the deepest pockets can gain any real experience of them.
When I first started being interested in wine in the mid nineties one could walk into any good wine shop and buy a proper bottle of really pleasure giving white burgundy without any difficulty at all. That is a million miles from the current reality and it has I’m afraid caused me almost to lose interest.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
To slightly bring it back to the original question - what does one do about this. It’s a discussion I’ve also had with Chet and I’m still willing to take bets on 1er crus for aging with producers who make wines in relatively traditional styles (without overwhelming reduction, for example). Will they all be premoxed in 30 years or not be as interesting as the wines made in the late 80s are now? I hope not! But, I’m OK with trying and drinking some of them along the way to see how they’re doing. And, in the meantime I can drink the younger drinking vintages like 2015. I am, however, much less willing to take those bets on grand crus, and the market pricing has made that an easier decision to make

As far as BdB Champagne - I love BdB champagne in particular, but don’t get the same thrill from 40 year old champagne as I do from white burgundy (though I do love older Comtes and Salon (speaking of variability…)). What somewhat concerns me is that I don’t know how the current grower champagnes will age. I think some will, but will the zero/super low dosage ones? I personally doubt it.

While I do understand the issue (obviously), it is a champagne and caviar problem - pun intended.

When I first started being interested in wine in the mid nineties one could walk into any good wine shop and buy a proper bottle of really pleasure giving white burgundy without any difficulty at all. That is a million miles from the current reality and it has I’m afraid caused me almost to lose interest.

I’ve seen this sentiment from a lot of people who started drinking Burgundy before the turn of the millenium and it always reminds me of fans of a band that has moved from playing dingy clubs to selling out massive stadiums. The band is harder and more expensive to see now, but that’s because everyone has caught on. A number of my friends love Jura, but if it continues on its upward price trajectory, I really hope I don’t have to hear in 10 years how in 2018 they could walk into any decent natural wine shop and buy Labet by the case.

I think there’s plenty of good Burgundy being made right now that’s enjoyable young, it’s just either expensive or needs a bit more information (which is certainly out there). But this just means Burgundy fans have won - their preferences have carried the day and as a result the best wines are now sought after world wide.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Any suggestions of producers today who are producing wines in the style of the “white Burgundy of yesteryear”? I started my wine journey firmly in the premox era so I don’t have a good sense of it.

Hard to answer because in various ways everyone has “cleaned things up”, and the fruit today isn’t the same as the fruit of the early 1990s. Some sort of change and evolution is natural, as children rarely want to drink or make exactly the same wines as their parents (the white Burgs of the 70s and 80s, allowing for the difference in age, are different in style form the white Burgs of the 30s and 40s). In a sense, looking at a totally unreconstructed producer such as Domaine Jean Javillier in Meursault would make sense if you want a very literal answer to your question. But if you permit me to answer more broadly, in the sense of producers making age-worthy, classically structured wines that need a bit of time to unwind and which gain a lot of complexity by age ten and more by age twenty, I would nominate, off the top of my head, Bernard Moreau, Henri Germain, Jean-Claude Bachelet, Hubert Lamy, Coche-Dury, Dureuil-Janthial, Lamy-Caillat, Pierre Morey, Guffens-Heynen and d’Auvenay. Within that short and non-exhaustive list, Lamy and Guffens are both using DIAM (Guffens since 2010, and Lamy bit by bit, and wholesale since 2017). I have confined myself to producers I own a reasonable amount of (with the exception of d’Auvenay), so that I am only giving you recommendations that I have followed myself.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
As for Dauvissat, I of course agree that the winemaking there, which now results in an incredibly high incidence of premature oxidation, has not changed in about a hundred years.

Hardly that. In the last 100 years, they went from basket to Vaslin to pneumatic press; from new feuillettes (selling the wine in the barrel) to domaine bottling and to old 228-liter barrels; and from bottling by hand direct from the barrel to a mechanical bottling line. At some point they also went over to vinifying in tank before barreling down, probably in the 1960s or 1970s.

Personally, if I had to speculate about the cause of premox chez Dauvissat, I would be looking at dissolved oxygen at bottling, low-ish SO2, and then the closure. Vincent does not travel and isn’t really aware he has a problem, given the wines from his cellar always seem to be just fine.

Sorry - I’m not sure I follow. Are you distinguishing between my hundred years and the fact that they Domaine bottled in the 1930s, which would only be 90 years? Or are you saying they didn’t Domaine bottle in the 1930s?

Your attention to detail prompted me to look up the dates in Khiem Le’s excellent book, where he quotes René Dauvissat. They began direct sales to the final client in 1931, but the wine was still shipped in bulk, not domaine bottled:

Wine was rarely bottled at the estate. Instead, it was sold in cask. “We ensured that the vinification of our wines was finished, before shipping them in > feuillettes chablisiennes > (132-liter barrels). Initially, the bottling took place on the customers’ premises—in restaurants, grocery stores, and wine shops. Subsequently, Parisian establishments had difficulty in executing this task because competent cellar hands became rare. As a result, customers started to buy bottled wine directly from us. And from 1950, improvements in road transportation allowed us to ship our wine in bottles, labeled as Domaine Robert Dauvissat, while continuing to send wine in feuillettes to Paris for another 15 years.”

“Several well-known French actors bought wine from us, but we had a much more illustrious customer. A Parisian wine merchant ordered a quarteau, or 66-liter barrel, of La Forest each year. We learned that his principal client was Monsignor Roncalli, the Apostolic Nuncio (the Vatican ambassador) to France from 1944 to 1953. He loved Chablis and served it to his guests. He then became Pope: John XXIII was Monseigneur Roncalli! We have always felt sure that it was thanks to the wine of Chablis, ‘which enlightens the spirit,’ that he had the idea to launch the Second Vatican Council!”

From a winemaking perspective, this is interesting because of the use of new feuillettes as opposed to the old 228-liter barrels of today.

Sorry - I’m not sure I follow. Are you distinguishing between my hundred years and the fact that they Domaine bottled in the 1930s, which would only be 90 years? Or are you saying they didn’t Domaine bottle in the 1930s?

Your attention to detail prompted me to look up the dates in Khiem Le’s excellent book, where he quotes René Dauvissat. They began direct sales to the final client in 1931, but the wine was still shipped in bulk, not domaine bottled:

Wine was rarely bottled at the estate. Instead, it was sold in cask. “We ensured that the vinification of our wines was finished, before shipping them in > feuillettes chablisiennes > (132-liter barrels). Initially, the bottling took place on the customers’ premises—in restaurants, grocery stores, and wine shops. Subsequently, Parisian establishments had difficulty in executing this task because competent cellar hands became rare. As a result, customers started to buy bottled wine directly from us. And from 1950, improvements in road transportation allowed us to ship our wine in bottles, labeled as Domaine Robert Dauvissat, while continuing to send wine in feuillettes to Paris for another 15 years.”

“Several well-known French actors bought wine from us, but we had a much more illustrious customer. A Parisian wine merchant ordered a quarteau, or 66-liter barrel, of La Forest each year. We learned that his principal client was Monsignor Roncalli, the Apostolic Nuncio (the Vatican ambassador) to France from 1944 to 1953. He loved Chablis and served it to his guests. He then became Pope: John XXIII was Monseigneur Roncalli! We have always felt sure that it was thanks to the wine of Chablis, ‘which enlightens the spirit,’ that he had the idea to launch the Second Vatican Council!”

From a winemaking perspective, this is interesting because of the use of new feuillettes as opposed to the old 228-liter barrels of today.

Interesting. I just checked Anthony Hanson (second edition): “They have been bottling since 1931, for grandfather Dauvissat loved his wines, and loved tasting them.”
Alex

Any suggestions of producers today who are producing wines in the style of the “white Burgundy of yesteryear”? I started my wine journey firmly in the premox era so I don’t have a good sense of it.

Hard to answer because in various ways everyone has “cleaned things up”, and the fruit today isn’t the same as the fruit of the early 1990s. Some sort of change and evolution is natural, as children rarely want to drink or make exactly the same wines as their parents (the white Burgs of the 70s and 80s, allowing for the difference in age, are different in style form the white Burgs of the 30s and 40s). In a sense, looking at a totally unreconstructed producer such as Domaine Jean Javillier in Meursault would make sense if you want a very literal answer to your question. But if you permit me to answer more broadly, in the sense of producers making age-worthy, classically structured wines that need a bit of time to unwind and which gain a lot of complexity by age ten and more by age twenty, I would nominate, off the top of my head, Bernard Moreau, Henri Germain, Jean-Claude Bachelet, Hubert Lamy, Coche-Dury, Dureuil-Janthial, Lamy-Caillat, Pierre Morey, Guffens-Heynen and d’Auvenay. Within that short and non-exhaustive list, Lamy and Guffens are both using DIAM (Guffens since 2010, and Lamy bit by bit, and wholesale since 2017). I have confined myself to producers I own a reasonable amount of (with the exception of d’Auvenay), so that I am only giving you recommendations that I have followed myself.

Thank you! Very interesting response and it includes some of my favorites.

Yes, Germain is an example of a producer that does pretty much everything right. But you can do everything right, and still be let down by the closure.

This is one of the more perplexing pieces to the puzzle because it still leaves us with the question of how the frequency of being let down by the closure increased exponentially starting almost immediately circa 1995. Certainly one would expect a producer doing everything right not to have any higher premox incidence than they did before 1995. You’d also expect that if hundreds of producers up and down the Cote simultaneously started doing different things circa 1995, it wouldn’t be so difficult to put a finger on what those things were, and then start, y’know, not doing them.

I think the best explanation is a decline in the quality of the closures (not necessarily visually, but in terms of density and OTR) happening at around the same time as winemaking changes made the wines a lot more fragile.

Basically a perfect storm of conditions leading to the phenomena, in a sense? Clearly it’s no easy task for even the “best” of growers and producers to eliminate the issue and while it seems like we may never see the wines of yesteryear again, its assuring to know that people are continuously innovating and evolving to define what modern white burgundy can be.

When I first started being interested in wine in the mid nineties one could walk into any good wine shop and buy a proper bottle of really pleasure giving white burgundy without any difficulty at all. That is a million miles from the current reality and it has I’m afraid caused me almost to lose interest.

I’ve seen this sentiment from a lot of people who started drinking Burgundy before the turn of the millenium and it always reminds me of fans of a band that has moved from playing dingy clubs to selling out massive stadiums. The band is harder and more expensive to see now, but that’s because everyone has caught on. A number of my friends love Jura, but if it continues on its upward price trajectory, I really hope I don’t have to hear in 10 years how in 2018 they could walk into any decent natural wine shop and buy Labet by the case.

I think there’s plenty of good Burgundy being made right now that’s enjoyable young, it’s just either expensive or needs a bit more information (which is certainly out there). But this just means Burgundy fans have won - their preferences have carried the day and as a result the best wines are now sought after world wide.

A really good point; it’s just that I don’t think it’s true!

White burgundy opinions expressed in this thread are painfully true. Lots of thoughtful effort and responses by many highly skilled and informed wine lovers.
The beverage “formally known as “ white burgundy should hereinafter be known as post -95 or post premox white burgundy. I happily purchase without hesitation closures like those used by the likes of Leeuwin for their Artist Series Chardonnay and Kumeu River for Mates, and don’t hesitate to set those down in my cellar. Others from around the edges of burgundy also, but for earlier consumption, and they are what they are and meant for earlier consumption with no risk for premox including great Maconnais as well as a few Oregon wines. For Oregon, we’ll see how those age over time. Oh and Kalin’s often rewarding Chardonnays from California. Those don’t fall apart either.

The remaining constant is that the worlds most pleasurable beverage has evolved into a different wine, and there exists little or no likelihood of acquiring a case or 2 like one could confidently do in the 80’s and early 90’s of Niellon Vergers, Ramonet Ruchottes, Sauzet Truffieres, Laguiche Montrachet, or Leflaive Combettes as a few examples and unflinchingly bury them all and each deep in the cellar and adhere to the rule I learned early on, “ drink em young or drink em old, but never in between. For me Chablis Clos from Dauvissat or Raveneau as examples will never replace or could not compete with the likes of George Deleger Chevalier, or Pierre or Bernard Morey Batard, or Jacques Gagnard’s Batard or Montrachet and that is the sad reality. And if I could acquire Raveneau at a price that wasn’t so insanely dizzying, I’d drink a bottle every day.

And Clos is NOT what it used to be either which is probably worth another thread and 100 responses as to a why not…

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I go back to my question. Have you had a premoxed white Burgundy finished with DIAM? The problem likely had a number of causes and likely was present before 1995, but in my somewhat limited experience with wines finished with DIAM, I have not had a premoxed wine.

No, but this merely shows that cork is an essential factor but not the sole factor, and it only solves the problem if producers aren’t too stubborn to switch to Diam, which, apparently, most are.

Absolutely.

I think one reason this debate gets so heated at times is that we are dealing with two separate but interrelated issues as if they were one. The first is the issue of oxidation, which can be solved by screwcaps and DIAM. The second is the issue of stylistic change, both because of winemaking and climate, which surely contributed to the oxidation issue, but also really changed the profile of the wines themselves. Anyone who is nostalgic for the glories of white Burgundy of yesteryear has to be interested in the second issue, which is in many respects more complicated, as it is not just a question of problem and solution.

Absolutely agree. But, the second one you cover in your tasting notes in the Wine Advocate. I wish you (and John Gilman) would list in your tasting notes for white Burgs whether the wines being reviewed are finished with corks, DIAM or screwcaps.
